description: The state of being complete, undivided, and intact in one's essential nature. Wholeness encompasses physical completeness, spiritual integrity, and the recognition that all parts are necessary for the health of the whole.
sound_symbolism: The vast breath 'who' encompasses all - like wind gathering every leaf, wholeness excludes nothing. This all-embracing quality opens through 'a', acknowledging that true wholeness includes space, includes possibility, includes what is yet to come. The vowel flow from round 'o' to open 'a' traces wholeness expanding - from perfect circle to infinite field. Two syllables hold completeness: the gathering breath and the boundless opening.
grammatical_notes: Functions as noun (wholeness), adjective (whole/complete), and verb (to become whole/to make whole) without morphological change, similar to Mandarin wánzhěng (完整). The verbal usage expresses the process of becoming complete or making something whole.
